content.text: Examples are the most reliable way to communicate format and style requirements to a language model. An instruction like "write in a casual tone" leaves the model guessing what casual means to you; two examples of casual tone show it exactly. This technique — providing input-output pairs in the prompt — is called few-shot prompting when applied to a whole task, but the same principle applies narrowly to format: even a single well-chosen example eliminates most format ambiguity. Examples are particularly powerful when the desired output has a structure that is hard to describe in words but easy to demonstrate.
One example fully conveys the structure
Two example sentences anchor the register exactly
Show the desired output for a missing-value input
Label definitions overlap and are hard to specify
Borderline examples show where the boundary is
Sufficient — model knows the task well
Examples add tokens but little extra signal

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def ask(prompt: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=150,
        temperature=0.0,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


# Without example: model guesses what "concise changelog entry" means
print(ask(
    "Write a concise changelog entry for: added dark mode toggle to settings page"
))
print()

# With example: format is unambiguous
print(ask("""
Write a concise changelog entry following the format below.

Example:
Input: fixed crash when uploading files over 100MB
Output: **Fix** — Resolved crash during upload of files exceeding 100 MB.

Now write one for:
Input: added dark mode toggle to settings page
Output:"""))
# → **Feature** — Added dark mode toggle to the Settings page.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def few_shot_classify(
    examples: list[tuple[str, str]],
    query: str,
    labels: list[str],
) -> str:
    """
    Build a few-shot classification prompt from (input, label) pairs.
    The pattern of examples teaches the model the label boundaries.
    """
    shots = "\n".join(
        f"Text: {text}\nLabel: {label}" for text, label in examples
    )
    prompt = (
        f"Classify the text. Valid labels: {', '.join(labels)}.\n\n"
        f"{shots}\n\n"
        f"Text: {query}\nLabel:"
    )
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


EXAMPLES = [
    ("The checkout process is smooth and intuitive.",           "Positive"),
    ("I've been waiting 3 weeks for a refund.",                "Negative"),
    ("Delivery was on time.",                                  "Neutral"),
    ("Absolutely love the new design — so clean!",             "Positive"),
    ("App crashes every time I open the camera.",              "Negative"),
]

TEST_CASES = [
    "The product is fine, does what it says.",
    "Customer support resolved my issue within an hour.",
    "Terrible experience. Would not recommend.",
]

for text in TEST_CASES:
    label = few_shot_classify(EXAMPLES, text, ["Positive", "Negative", "Neutral"])
    print(f"{label:10s}  {text}")
If your task has three classes, include at least one example of each. A skewed set of examples biases the model toward the over-represented class.
The most useful examples are the ones near the decision boundary — they teach the model where one label ends and another begins.
Use the same delimiter pattern ("Text:", "Label:") in every example and in the query. Inconsistency causes the model to follow the format of the last example it saw.
Models give more weight to recently seen tokens. The query should always be the last item, immediately before where the model generates.
3–5 examples is usually the sweet spot.
More examples improve robustness but consume context tokens. Beyond 8–10 examples, returns diminish; consider fine-tuning instead.

An example is worth a hundred words of specification. When you cannot easily describe a rule but you can recognise the right answer when you see it, demonstrating it in the prompt is both faster and more reliable than trying to articulate it. The constraint is token budget: each example costs context space, so choose examples that carry the most information — specifically, those that cover boundaries and edge cases your instruction text cannot capture.
